title: Quiz: Infrastructure as Code for Quantum Systems
description: Test your understanding of Infrastructure as Code practices for quantum computing environments

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of using Infrastructure as Code for quantum computing environments?

options.text: It makes quantum algorithms run faster
It enables version-controlled, reproducible, and auditable infrastructure deployments
It reduces the number of qubits needed
It is required by quantum hardware providers

questions.explanation: IaC treats infrastructure configuration as software, enabling version control, code review, testing, and automated deployment. This ensures environments are reproducible and changes are auditable through git history.

questions.question: In Terraform, what is the purpose of a 'module'?

options.text: To store quantum backend credentials
To encapsulate reusable infrastructure components that can be shared across environments
To run quantum simulations
To compress Terraform state files

questions.explanation: Terraform modules encapsulate related resources into reusable components. A module for a 'quantum-cluster' can be used across dev, staging, and production environments with different configurations.

questions.question: How should quantum backend credentials (like IBM Quantum tokens) be handled in Terraform?

options.text: Hardcode them directly in .tf files for convenience
Store them in a separate credentials.txt file in the repo
Use secret management services (AWS Secrets Manager) and pass values via environment variables or encrypted tfvars
Credentials are not needed for Terraform

questions.explanation: Sensitive values should never be committed to version control. Use secret management services like AWS Secrets Manager and inject values via environment variables (TF_VAR_*) or encrypted tfvars files.

questions.question: What is GitOps in the context of infrastructure management?

options.text: Using Git to store quantum circuits
A workflow where Git is the single source of truth for infrastructure, and changes are applied through pull requests
A Git plugin for Terraform
A method to compress Git repositories

questions.explanation: GitOps uses Git as the single source of truth for both application code and infrastructure. Changes are proposed via pull requests, reviewed, and automatically applied when merged, providing a complete audit trail.

questions.question: Why might you use Pulumi instead of Terraform for quantum infrastructure?

options.text: Pulumi is the only tool that supports quantum computers
Pulumi allows writing infrastructure in Python, which may be more natural for quantum developers already using Python
Terraform does not support AWS
Pulumi runs quantum circuits

questions.explanation: Pulumi supports general-purpose programming languages including Python. For quantum developers already using Python with Qiskit or other frameworks, writing infrastructure in Python can be more natural than learning Terraform's HCL.

questions.question: Which of the following are security best practices for quantum infrastructure? (Select all that apply)

options.text: Enable encryption at rest for secrets
Use network policies to isolate quantum workloads
Store all credentials in plaintext for easy access
Implement automatic credential rotation

questions.explanation: Security best practices include encryption at rest, network isolation via Kubernetes network policies, and automatic credential rotation. Storing credentials in plaintext is a security anti-pattern.

questions.question: What is the purpose of terraform plan in a CI/CD pipeline?

options.text: To immediately apply all changes
To preview what changes Terraform will make without applying them, allowing review
To delete all existing infrastructure
To validate Python syntax

questions.explanation: terraform plan shows what changes would be made without actually making them. In CI/CD, this is typically run on pull requests so reviewers can see the infrastructure impact before approving.

questions.question: When provisioning GPU nodes for quantum simulation, what Kubernetes mechanism prevents non-GPU workloads from being scheduled on expensive GPU nodes?

options.text: Labels only
Taints and tolerations
Node names
Pod priorities

questions.explanation: Taints on nodes prevent pods from being scheduled unless they have matching tolerations. GPU nodes are tainted so only pods that explicitly tolerate (and need) GPUs are scheduled there, preventing waste of expensive resources.
